frames.caption: Base case: dp[0] = 1 (empty prefix decodes one way) and dp[1] = 1 (first digit '2' is 1..9). prev2 = dp[0] = 1, prev1 = dp[1] = 1.
frames.note: State: dp[i] = the number of ways to decode the first i characters of s. The base case dp[0] = 1 means the empty prefix has exactly one decoding (the empty one) — forgetting this base case is THE classic Decode Ways bug, because every later count is built from it. dp[1] = 1 too, since a valid non-zero first character decodes exactly one way. The reviewer collapses this whole table into two rolling variables (prev2 = dp[i-2], prev1 = dp[i-1]) for O(1) space.

frames.caption: s[1]='2' is 1..9: it stands alone as a letter, so add dp[1] = 1. cur = 1.
frames.note: First transition: can s[i-1] stand alone as a one-letter code? A single digit decodes iff it is 1..9 — i.e. NOT '0', since no letter maps to 0. When it is valid, every way of decoding the first i-1 characters extends by this lone letter, so we add dp[i-1] (the cell to the immediate left). A standalone '0' contributes nothing here, which is exactly why "30"-style inputs collapse toward 0.

frames.caption: Pair s[0..1]="22" = 22 is in 10..26: it is one letter, so add dp[0] = 1. cur = 2.
frames.note: Second transition: can s[i-2..i-1] be read as a single two-letter code? The pair is valid iff it falls in 10..26 (J..Z) — note 27+ and anything like "06" are out, because a leading zero is not a real two-digit code. When valid, every decoding of the first i-2 characters extends by this one letter, so we add dp[i-2] (the cell two to the left). Summing the one-digit and two-digit contributions is the whole recurrence.

frames.caption: Commit dp[2] = 2. Roll the window: prev2 = 1, prev1 = 2.
frames.note: dp[2] now holds the number of ways to decode the first 2 characters. The two rolling variables slide right by one so the next iteration reads them as dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] again — that sliding window is what lets two ints replace the whole array.

frames.caption: s[2]='6' is 1..9: it stands alone as a letter, so add dp[2] = 2. cur = 2.
frames.note: First transition: can s[i-1] stand alone as a one-letter code? A single digit decodes iff it is 1..9 — i.e. NOT '0', since no letter maps to 0. When it is valid, every way of decoding the first i-1 characters extends by this lone letter, so we add dp[i-1] (the cell to the immediate left). A standalone '0' contributes nothing here, which is exactly why "30"-style inputs collapse toward 0.

frames.caption: Pair s[1..2]="26" = 26 is in 10..26: it is one letter, so add dp[1] = 1. cur = 3.
frames.note: Second transition: can s[i-2..i-1] be read as a single two-letter code? The pair is valid iff it falls in 10..26 (J..Z) — note 27+ and anything like "06" are out, because a leading zero is not a real two-digit code. When valid, every decoding of the first i-2 characters extends by this one letter, so we add dp[i-2] (the cell two to the left). Summing the one-digit and two-digit contributions is the whole recurrence.

frames.caption: Commit dp[3] = 3. Roll the window: prev2 = 2, prev1 = 3.
frames.note: dp[3] now holds the number of ways to decode the first 3 characters. The two rolling variables slide right by one so the next iteration reads them as dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] again — that sliding window is what lets two ints replace the whole array.

frames.caption: dp[3] = 3 — "226" decodes 3 ways: {2,2,6}=BBF, {22,6}=VF, {2,26}=BZ.
frames.note: Each dp cell was computed once from its two left neighbours, so the sweep is O(n) time. Because only dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] are ever read, the table compresses to two rolling variables — the O(1)-space form the reviewer ships. The final cell dp[n] is the answer: the number of distinct decodings of the whole string. Decode Ways is the string-DP cousin of Climbing Stairs — same "add the last one or two steps" shape, with validity guards replacing the unconditional +1.
